sections.content:

Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 37, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The level is not completed. Let me check the current state and think about what else might be needed. The green dot is now at offset (2, 2) from the gray box top-left, matching the yellow box position. But the level is still not complete.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics. Maybe the goal is not just about the green dot position, but also about the left panel configuration.
Looking at the current state:
- Both right panel shapes have no outlines (clicked)
- Red bar is horizontal
- Blue block is at bottom, checkered pattern at top
- Green dot is at offset (2, 2)
Maybe I need to click the blue shape again to swap back the blue block and checkered pattern to their initial positions. Or maybe I need to have the red bar vertical.
Let me try clicking the blue shape to swap back, and then see if the level completes.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the game entirely. The right panel shapes have white outlines initially. Clicking them removes the outlines. The left panel has colored blocks that change when shapes are clicked. The green dot can be moved.
Maybe the game is about "painting" the left panel to match the right panel shapes. The right panel shapes show the target configuration, and the left panel needs to be arranged to match them.
But the left panel and right panel have different sizes and structures, so this doesn't make sense directly.
Let me think about this differently. The right panel shapes have:
- Red shape: narrow top (7 wide), wider bottom (13 wide)
- Blue shape: same structure
The left panel has colored blocks that can be rearranged. Maybe the goal is to arrange the left panel blocks to form a similar "narrow top, wide bottom" structure.
Looking at the current left panel:
- Checkered pattern at rows 20-23, cols 18-21 (4x4)
- Red bar horizontal at rows 30-33, cols 12-17 (4x6)
- Dark red bar at rows 30-33, cols 18-21 (4x4)
- Gray box at rows 30-33, cols 8-11 (4x4)
- Blue block at rows 34-37, cols 8-11 (4x4)
The red bar is horizontal (4x6), and the dark red bar is 4x4. Together, they form a 4x10 horizontal strip at rows 30-33. The gray box is 4x4 at rows 30-33, cols 8-11. So the middle section (rows 30-33) has a 4x18 horizontal strip (gray box + red bar + dark red bar).
The top section (rows 20-23) has a 4x4 checkered pattern and a 4x4 yellow box (in the top gray box).
The bottom section (rows 34-37) has a 4x4 blue block.
This doesn't directly match the right panel shapes' "narrow top, wide bottom" structure.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe the goal is to click the blue shape to swap back the blue block and checkered pattern, and then move the green dot to a different position.
Actually, I just realized something. The initial state had:
- Blue square at rows 20-23, cols 18-21
- Red bar horizontal at rows 30-33, cols 12-17
- Checkered pattern at rows 34-37, cols 8-11
- Green dot at rows 40-41, cols 10-11
The current state has:
- Checkered pattern at rows 20-23, cols 18-21
- Red bar horizontal at rows 30-33, cols 12-17
- Blue block at rows 34-37, cols 8-11
- Green dot at rows 40-41, cols 10-11 (approximately)
The main difference is that the blue square and checkered pattern have swapped positions. Maybe the goal is to have them in the initial positions (blue square at top, checkered at bottom).
Let me click the blue shape to swap them back.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check green dot position first
for node in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"Green dot: boundary={node['boundary']}")

# Click blue shape to swap back blue block and checkered pattern
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 48}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
